=== YOUR PROBLEM ===
UUID: java-lld-parking-001
TITLE: Design a Parking Lot
DOMAIN: parking
PROBLEM: Design an in-memory, thread-safe parking lot: multiple floors, spots of different sizes (compact/large/handicapped), vehicles park and unpark, find nearest available spot, charge by time. Focus on: (1) spot allocation strategy (best-fit by size), (2) concurrent park/unpark without double-allocation.

CONCURRENCY ANGLE (MUST realize in code + §9):
PER-SPOT FINE-GRAINED LOCKS: each ParkingSpot has its own ReentrantLock. park() acquires spot.lock() before marking occupied → prevents double-allocation race. Multiple threads can park/unpark different spots concurrently (no global bottleneck). Concurrency test: N threads park at once, assert no two get same spot.

14	## Operational Facts (Java-Specific)
15	
16	### Workspace Structure
17	```
18	/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/<UUID>_lld/
19	  pom.xml

41

49	1. Replace `<artifactId>DOMAIN</artifactId>` with actual domain

51

54	- ✅ `src/main/java/com/interview/lld/resourcepool/model/Model.java`
55	- ✅ `src/main/java/com/interview/lld/resourcepool/service/ResourcePoolService.java`

61	### Java 17+ Idioms
62	- **Types**: `List<Foo>`, `Map<K, V>`, `Set<T>`, `@Nullable X`, `Optional<X>` for return types
63	- **Immutable entities**: `record Foo(int id, String name) {}` (Java 17+)
64	- **Exceptions**: Extend `RuntimeException` (unchecked), one base per domain
65	- **Concurrency**:
66	  - `ReentrantLock` (java.util.concurrent.locks)
67	  - `Semaphore` (java.util.concurrent)
68	  - `CyclicBarrier` (java.util.concurrent)
69	  - Lock pattern: `lock.lock(); try { ... } finally { lock.unlock(); }`
70	- **Threading**: `ExecutorService exec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(N); exec.submit(() -> ...); exec.shutdown(); exec.awaitTermination(...);`
71	
72	### Testing (JUnit 5)
73	```java
74	import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
75	import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;
76	
77	class ResourcePoolServiceTest {
78	    @Test
79	    void testAcquire() {
80	        var pool = new ResourcePoolService(...);
81	        var resource = pool.acquire(Duration.ofSeconds(5));
82	        assertNotNull(resource);
83	    }
84	    
85	    @Test
86	    void testExhaustion() {
87	        var pool = new ResourcePoolService(..., maxSize: 1);
88	        pool.acquire(null);
89	        assertThrows(PoolExhaustedException.class, () -> pool.acquire(Duration.ofMillis(50)));
90	    }
91	}
92	```
93	
94	### Concurrency Test (Strict)
95	```java
96	int N = 50, CAP = 5;
97	CyclicBarrier barrier = new CyclicBarrier(N);
98	AtomicInteger served = new AtomicInteger(0);
99	AtomicInteger peak = new AtomicInteger(0);
100	Set<String> distinct = ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet();
101	
102	ExecutorService exec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(N);
103	for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
104	    exec.submit(() -> {
105	        try {
106	            barrier.await();  // stampede
107	            try (var r = pool.acquire(Duration.ofSeconds(10))) {
108	                served.incrementAndGet();
109	                distinct.add(r.getPayload().toString());
110	                int cur = pool.inUseCount();
111	                peak.updateAndGet(p -> Math.max(p, cur));
112	                assertTrue(cur <= CAP, "capacity breached");
113	                Thread.sleep(20);  // hold to force genuine concurrency
114	            }
115	        } catch (Exception e) { throw new RuntimeException(e); }
116	    });
117	}
118	exec.shutdown();
119	assertTrue(exec.awaitTermination(15, TimeUnit.SECONDS));
120	
121	assertEquals(N, served.get(), "all threads served");
122	assertEquals(CAP, peak.get(), "pool actually filled to capacity");
123	assertTrue(distinct.size() <= CAP, "no more than CAP resources created");
124	```
125

132	- **§9 concurrency**: Template Method hooks (base class: no-op `protected void gateLock() {}`, thread-safe subclass: `@Override protected void gateLock() { lock.lock(); }`)
133	
134	## Mermaid Rules (CRITICAL)
135	- Exactly TWO blocks: ```mermaid classDiagram (§4.2) and ```mermaid sequenceDiagram (§8)
136	- **NO SEMICOLONS** in sequenceDiagram message text — mermaid parser fails on `;`. Write "do A then B", NOT "A; B".
137	
138	## Output Artifacts
139	
140	1. **Workspace files** (via Write tool): pom.xml + all `.java` files with full paths
141	2. **`_response.md`** in workspace root: the FULL §1–§9 markdown (NOT abbreviated)
142	3. **DO NOT** assemble JSON — orchestrator does that
143	
144	## Before Finishing
145	
146	Run these and confirm green:
147	```bash
148	cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/<UUID>_lld
149	mvn compile exec:java -q   # Main.java all cases print OK
150	mvn test -q                # JUnit suite: N passed, 0 failures
151	```
152	
153	If red, fix CODE (not test) and rerun. Update §7.1 / §7.2 in `_response.md` with actual output.
154	
155	## Distinct Concurrency Angle (Per-Problem)
156	
157	Each problem must realize a DIFFERENT concurrency mechanism (no two use the exact same pattern):
158	- Lock ordering (deadlock-free)
159	- Lock striping (per-customer shards)
160	- Permit-guarded gate (Semaphore + lazy creation)
161	- Idempotent dedup (concurrent seen-set, work outside lock)
162	- Fair FIFO (Semaphore + queue)
163	- Optimistic CAS loop
164	- Per-resource locks (fine-grained)
165	
166	---
167

The thread-safe version is a subclass that only overrides this one method. The diff is about 10 lines." 129 130 Use when: any problem that asks about concurrency (which is every LLD problem). 131 132 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 133 MOVE 3: Lock the Map Before You Lock the Thing 134 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 135 If you keep one lock per resource in a dictionary, lock the dictionary itself before reading from it — using a separate lock. 136 137 Why this matters: two threads creating a new key in a dict at the same time can corrupt the dict itself. Without this, threads just take turns — there is no real race. 173 174 ```python 175 N = 50 176 start = threading.Barrier(N) # all N threads wait here together 177 178 def attempt(user): 179 session = svc.start_session(user, resource) 180 start.wait() # wait until all N are ready, then go 181 try: 182 svc.claim(session.id, ["A1"]) # now all N hit at the same time 183 except ResourceNotAvailable: 184 pass 185 ``` 186 187 What to say: "The Barrier makes all 50 threads hit the critical part at the exact same moment. Never invent new filenames. This is the CLEAN, single-threaded-correct CORE — do NOT add any locks or thread-safety here; concurrency hardening is a deliberate SECOND version in §9. Keep §6/§7 lock-free and readable so the OO design is the star; §7's run proves FUNCTIONAL correctness only. 780 781 PRESENTATION — METHOD BY METHOD, not class by class. Inside the orchestrator, do NOT dump the whole class as one ```java block. Instead, for the orchestrator: 782 1. First show `__init__` + the method signatures (skeleton, with `...` bodies for the methods you'll fill in below). This is the API tour — interviewer sees the full surface area in one glance. 783 2. Then for EACH non-trivial method, present its OWN small ```java block followed by the 4-block format below (role / code / 🎙️ narration / ⚠️ follow-up). 784 785 The skeleton-then-flesh approach matches how the candidate would actually write the class at a whiteboard, and it makes the rendered output scannable instead of one wall of code. Small entity classes (`Compartment`, `Package`, `Reservation`, `Locker`) can still be presented as a single ```java block per file because their methods are short and tightly coupled — the per-method split is only required for the orchestrator. 786 787 **LIVE WRITING ORDER — the candidate's playbook for the live interview itself.** 788 789 This is the BIG mental-model section. The candidate cannot write the §6 code in any order they want — there's a specific phased sequence that maximises interviewer signal and minimises wasted time. ALWAYS render this as a sub-section titled "### 6.0 Live Writing Order (your interview playbook)" BEFORE any code blocks. The candidate reads this OUT LOUD pacing in their head while writing — it's their clock. The snippets are illustrative TEMPLATES adapted to THIS problem (Locker / ParkingLot / etc.) — not full re-renders of §6 code. 796 797 **Phase 1 — Set the stage (1-2 min). NO CODE YET.** 798 > Bolo first: "Main `.java`, `.java`, aur `.java` — ye 3 > files banaunga. Pehle states define karunga (enums), phir `` > ki state machine, phir orchestrator. Code likhne se pehle main aapko har class ka > role bata deta hoon." 799 > Why this matters: 30 seconds of framing tells the interviewer you're not in > chaos. Roadmap pehle, code baad mein. 800 801 **Phase 2 — Enums first (2 min). Quick win.** 802 > Bolo while writing: "States pehle — har class ki vocabulary yahaan se aati hai. > `IntEnum` use kar raha hoon size ke liye taaki 'fits' check ek line ho jaaye." 803 > Likho: 1 small ```java block with the IntEnum + 1-2 plain Enums. No methods. 804 > Why this matters: psychological — first green checkmark on the board, momentum > established. 805 806 **Phase 3 — Most-interesting entity state machine (5 min).** 807 > Bolo: "Ye system ka heart hai. Sirf yeh class apna status badal sakti hai — > orchestrator kabhi seedha status set nahi karega. Isse illegal jumps impossible > ho jaate hain by construction." 808 > Likho: the entity dataclass + every state-transition method (`reserve`, `release`, > `occupy` for Locker; `hold`, `book`, `cancel` for Seat; etc.). Idempotent guards > shown explicitly. 809 > Then immediately drop the **PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG**: "Yahan ek race > condition hogi `` mein — main usko §9 mein per-resource lock se > fix karunga. Abhi clean version likhta hoon." This single sentence is the > biggest senior signal in the whole interview. 810 811 **Phase 4 — Strategy interface + 1 concrete (1-2 min).** 812 > Bolo: "Strategy seam — orchestrator ko nahi pata best-fit hai ya nearest-door." 813 > Likho: ABC + abstractmethod + 1 concrete impl (~6 lines). 814 > If the design has no Strategy pattern, SKIP this phase entirely — don't fabricate one. 815 816 **Phase 5 — Orchestrator SKELETON (2-3 min).** ⚠️ CRITICAL. 817 > Bolo: "Pehle main saari API surface dikha deta hoon. Phir do main methods bharta hoon." 818 > Likho: ONLY `__init__` (with all fields, all injected dependencies) + EVERY public > method signature with `pass` body. Do NOT fill any body yet. 819 > Why this matters: interviewer sees the full API in one glance. If the candidate > dives straight into `assign_package`'s body, the interviewer waits anxiously > wondering what other methods exist. Skeleton answers that question once and for all. 820 821 **Phase 6 — Most important method, FULL body (5-7 min). Slow down here.** 822 > Bolo step-by-step (this is the most important 5 min of the interview). Walk > through guard clauses → critical block → notify-outside-lock → return. 823 > Likho: the full body of the orchestrator's pipeline method (`assign_package`, > `park`, `bookSeats`, `addExpense`). Include the no-op `_lock` context manager > marking the §9 boundary. Notify call OUTSIDE the lock. 824 > Narration must call out: "Notify lock ke BAAHAR hai — agar SMS slow hai to lock > mat block karo." This one sentence is a senior signal. 825 826 **Phase 7 — Second method, FULL body (3-4 min).** 827 > Bolo: "Ye redemption / completion path hai. guards, phir release." 828 > Likho: full body of `pickup_package` / `unpark` / `settle` etc. Reference §5.5.B > pseudocode 1:1 — no improvisation, just translation. 829 830 **Phase 8 — Demo verbally OR run (3-4 min).** 831 > Mode A (Amazon-style, no execution): "Main 3 cases verbally walk karunga: > happy path, capacity-full, edge case." Then SHOW state transitions in plain > text on the board: 832 > ``` 833 > assign(SMALL pkg) → C1: AVAILABLE → RESERVED, _by_code={"9421": res-1} 834 > deposit(res-1) → C1: RESERVED → OCCUPIED 835 > pickup("9421") → C1: OCCUPIED → AVAILABLE, _by_code={} 836 > ``` 837 > Mode B (Uber/Meta coding env): `mvn compile exec:java -Dexec.mainClass="com.interview.lld..Main" -q`, show actual stdout. 838 > Wait until ALL methods are full-body before this step. A demo with one half-written > method is worse than no demo. 839 840 **Phase 9 — Concurrency (2-3 min, ONLY if asked).** 841 > Bolo first, ALWAYS: "Race `` mein hai — do callers same resource > dekh ke dono reserve kar dete hain. Fix: per-resource RLock through Template > Method — `_lock` hook ko subclass override karta hai, core code zero changes." 842 > Likho the thread-safe subclass ONLY if interviewer says "show me the code". > Otherwise stay verbal. 843 844 #### (b) The 7 golden rules — must be rendered as a numbered list 845 846 1. **Roadmap pehle bolo.** "Main 3 files banaunga, A → B → C." 30 seconds of talking saves the interviewer's anxiety and frames everything that follows. 847 848 2. **Skeleton-then-flesh.** Orchestrator gets `pass` bodies first, full bodies later. They're independent; orchestrator imports them. 851 852 4. **BOLO while likhing.** Silence = interviewer's brain wanders. Even a one-liner helps: "yahan clock inject kar raha hoon", "yeh guard rejection path hai". 853 854 5. **Concurrency flag drop karo entity ke baad.** Proactively, before the interviewer asks. Single biggest senior signal of the whole hour. 855 856 6. **Demo wait karo.** Even one half-written method = no demo. Show progress only once everything runs. 857 858 7. **Pseudocode → code 1:1.** §5.5.B's numbered logic becomes §6's code in the SAME order. Don't improvise live — translate the dry-run. 859 860 #### (c) TL;DR time budget table — render exactly this shape 861 862 | Time | Phase | What you're writing | 863 |---|---|---| 864 | 0-2 min | Roadmap | Just talking, no code | 865 | 2-4 min | Enums | Full enums | 866 | 4-9 min | State-machine entity | Full state machine class + methods | 867 | 9-11 min | Strategy seam | ABC + 1 concrete (skip if no Strategy in §5) | 868 | 11-14 min | Orchestrator skeleton | `__init__` + signatures with `pass` | 869 | 14-21 min | Pipeline method (`assign` / `park` / `book`) | Full body + narration | 870 | 21-25 min | Redemption method (`pickup` / `unpark` / `settle`) | Full body + narration | 871 | 25-29 min | Demo / verbal trace | 3 cases |

872	| 29-32 min | Concurrency answer | Verbal first, code only if asked |
873	
874	Total: ~30 minutes of code time. In a 60-min interview, allocate 25 min for §1-§5 (talking + diagram), 30 min for §6 code, 5 min for follow-ups.
875	
876	#### (d) Mid-flight situations — when things don't go to plan
877	
878	Every interview hits at least one of these. The candidate must know the response before the situation arises — improvising under pressure usually makes it worse. Render this as a numbered list of 5 situations, each with a one-line response.
879	
880	  1. **Interviewer interrupts mid-method.** STOP writing. Don't try to talk and write simultaneously — both will suffer. Address the question fully, then say "OK, where was I — I was in the middle of `<method>`, let me finish the lock block." Resume from the same line. Common mistake: keeping the cursor moving while answering → you'll write something nonsensical.
881	
882	  2. **Running out of time at the 40-min mark.** Cut in this EXACT order, never skip an earlier item to preserve a later one:
883	  > - **First cut:** second method body — describe verbally instead. "I'll describe `<second_method>` instead of writing it — same shape, three guards then release."
884	  > - **Second cut:** §6 demo / verbal trace — skip if you've narrated dry-runs in §5.5.
885	  > - **Third cut:** §9 code — stay verbal, never write the thread-safe subclass under time pressure.
886	  > - **NEVER cut:** state machine, pipeline method body, proactive concurrency flag. These three ARE the senior signal — losing any one costs more than overrunning by 2-3 min.
887	
888	  3. **Realized you need a missing field / method mid-write.** Pause for 3 seconds, voice it out loud — "actually I need a `created_at` on Reservation, let me add it" — then add it inline. DO NOT scroll back and silently edit; interviewer reads that as confusion. Voicing it reads as deliberate refinement.
889	
890	  4. **Interviewer says "what if X?" while you're mid-method.** Two valid responses, pick by scope:
891	  > - **Defer:** "Good question — let me finish this method, then show how the design handles that." Use when X is a §9 / follow-up topic.
892	  > - **Pivot inline:** "Actually that changes things — let me add a parameter here." Use when X is a small inline tweak (one arg, one status).
893	  > NEVER just say "yes that works" without knowing why — you'll paint yourself into a corner one method later.
894	
895	  5. **Interviewer silence.** Don't fill it with chatter — keep working at a steady pace. At natural breakpoints (after a method's full body), pause and ask: "any thoughts before I move to the next method?" Gives them a clean opening to redirect, without breaking your flow.
896	
897	End the §6.0 sub-section with a one-line takeaway:
898	> "Yahi flow practice karo. Sequence muscle-memory ban jaayegi: > entity → strategy → skeleton → main method → second method → demo. > Interview mein flow natural lagega."
899	
900	#### After §6.0 — what NOT to write
901	
902	🗣️ **DESCRIBE (don't write unless asked):** Simple entity classes (Show, Movie, Theatre, User, Ticket), simple factory/helper methods, basic exception classes. Say: "I also have [ClassName] — it just holds [fields], nothing interesting there."
903	
904	❓ **IF ASKED (write only if interviewer specifically requests it):** Thread-safe wrapper, sweep_expired/cleanup method, observer registration, main.java driver. These are §9 territory — don't pre-emptively write them.
905	
906	NARRATION ORDER — always follow the live writing order in §6.0 AND the §5.5-F roadmap. Open §6 with the one-liner 🎙️ Script from §5.5-F so the interviewer immediately knows the roadmap.
907	
908	For each ✍️ WRITE method (on the orchestrator), present ALL SIX of these in order — this is the mandatory tutorial-style format. Hellointerview reads as if a senior engineer is THINKING ALOUD; our output must read the same way.
909	
910	  **1. Role sentence:** One sentence — WHY this method exists, not just what it does.   "`depositPackage` is the core deposit workflow — it ties a compartment to a token   in one atomic step."
911	
912	  **2. Core logic (bulleted happy path):** A small numbered list of the steps the   method takes on the happy path, BEFORE any code appears. The reader scans this and   is mentally ready by the time they see the code. Format:
913	  > Core logic:
914	  > 1. Find an available compartment of the requested size
915	  > 2. Generate an access token for that compartment
916	  > 3. Mark the compartment as occupied
917	  > 4. Store the access token in the lookup map
918	  > 5. Return the access token code
919	
920	  **3. Edge cases (bulleted):** A small list of what can go wrong, written as nouns:
921	  > Edge cases:
922	  > - No compartment available of the requested size
923	  > - Invalid size parameter
924	  > - Code generated already exists (collision)
925	
926	  **4. Code block:** ```java — implementing EXACTLY the §5.5-B pseudocode.   ⚠️ NO `...` or `pass` as body placeholders anywhere in §6 code — every method must   have a real, complete, runnable body. Comment the one or two non-obvious lines inline   using `# WHY:`. Don't comment obvious lines.
927	
928	  **5. Reasoning paragraph (PROSE, not teleprompter):** 3-6 sentences of plain-English   explanation written as a senior engineer reasoning aloud — NOT a "say this out loud"   script. This is the longest piece in the method's section and must include:
929	  - **Open** with what this method is doing at a high level — one sentence — and refer to the most-interesting line by name (`compartment.open()` triggers the unlock; the `pop` burns the one-time code).
930	  - **At least one "Notice" sentence** about a deliberate non-decision — "Notice we're NOT checking if size is valid here — that's the job of `getAvailableCompartment`, which will scan and return None for unknown sizes." Senior signal: explicitly named choices.
931	  - **Production-vs-interview boundary** when applicable — "We assume the hardware auto-closes the door after ~30 seconds. In production we'd add physical sensors to verify the package is present before issuing the token; that adds state-management complexity beyond this interview's scope."
932	  - **The one tricky line** with reasoning — "The `pop` is what makes the code one-time. If we just left it in the map, a replay attack would work the second time. We delete on success AND in `_expire`, so the code is gone in either path."
933	
934	  This paragraph is what makes the section TEACHABLE, not just complete. It reads like hellointerview's prose, not like a code comment.
935	
936	  **6. 🎙️ Out-loud script + ⚠️ Follow-up:** Two short pieces — a 1-2 sentence condensed version of the reasoning paragraph for the candidate to MEMORIZE for the interview, plus the one follow-up question they'll be asked.
937	
938	  > 🎙️ Say: "Lookup, expiry guard, ready guard, then release. The one-time-ness is   > the pop — the code is gone, so it can never be replayed."
939	  > ⚠️ "What if the user enters the code right as it expires?" → "The expiry check   > runs first and calls `_expire`, which reclaims the box and drops the code.   > No window where a stale code still opens a box."
940	
941	The 🎙️ line is DERIVED from the reasoning paragraph — same content, condensed. They are not redundant; the paragraph teaches, the 🎙️ remembers.
942	
943	**APPROACH COMPARISON — for the ONE most interesting helper method only:**
944	Pick the single helper method where the candidate's design has a non-obvious choice (usually `getAvailableCompartment` / `findSpot` / `assignSeat` — the "how do I locate the right resource" method). For THIS one method, after blocks 1-4 above, insert an "### Approach" subsection that walks through 2-3 candidate implementations and EXPLICITLY rejects the first ones. Format strictly:
945	
946	  **Approach 1 — [name] (rejected):**   Pseudocode for the naive version.   > **Challenges:** [the SPECIFIC failure mode that kills this approach — not "it's slow", but "tokens expire 7 days before they're cleaned up, so during that window the compartment looks occupied for assignment but is actually free for new tokens — state divergence"]
947	
948	  **Approach 2 — [name] (rejected for this scale):**   Pseudocode for an over-engineered version (e.g. an indexed `Map<Size, Queue>`).   > **Challenges:** [the SPECIFIC complexity cost — "state lives in two places, synchronisation risk: forget to enqueue on pickup → compartment vanishes from availability"]. Add a one-line rule: "I'd flip to this when [N crosses threshold]."
949	
950	  **Chosen approach — [name]:**   Pseudocode for the one we're going with.   > **Why it wins:** [the SPECIFIC reason — "single source of truth: the compartment owns its own physical state, no synchronisation needed, scan is O(n) but n is tiny"].
951	
952	This subsection is the SENIOR SIGNAL. It shows the candidate considered alternatives and rejected them with reasons — the rule of thumb the interviewer scores on. Only do this for ONE method per problem (the most interesting helper) — more than one becomes noise.
953	
954	**HELPER METHODS — written, not skipped:**
955	After the public methods of the orchestrator, write a small ### subsection for the private helpers (`generateAccessToken`, `clearDeposit`, `_expire`, `_unique_code`). Each gets:
956	  - Role one-liner
957	  - Code block (often 3-5 lines)
958	  - One sentence of prose if the helper has a non-obvious choice (e.g. "Setting expiration to `now + 7.days()` here; the clock is injected so tests can fast-forward")
959	
960	Don't skip these — readers can't run code that doesn't exist. But don't over-explain either; helpers are short for a reason.
961	
962	**SMALL ENTITY CLASSES — single block per file:**
963	Entities like `Compartment`, `Reservation`, `Locker`, `Package` can be presented as a SINGLE ```java block per file (in `model/*.java`) with inline comments — they don't need the 6-block format. The 6-block treatment is for the orchestrator's PUBLIC methods and the one approach-comparison helper.
964	
965	Cover the enums, the interfaces/abstract base classes, the strategies, and the orchestrator. EVERY class shown here must appear in the §4 diagram (and vice-versa).
966	
967	**VERIFICATION — full-lifecycle dry runs (after the orchestrator, before §7):**
968	After all methods are presented, add a "### Verification" subsection that traces 3 scenarios with EXPLICIT before/after state tables. Hellointerview's verification section is the strongest interview signal because it proves the design works on paper before any test runs. Three scenarios:
969	
970	  1. **Happy-path lifecycle** — `assign → deposit → pickup`. Show the state of every data structure (`compartments`, `_reservations`, `_by_code`) before each step and after each step. Use a small table or indented `State:` lines per step.
971	
972	  2. **An expired-pickup attempt** — `assign → deposit → fast-forward clock → pickup`. Show that `pickup` raises and the compartment is correctly reclaimed by `_expire`.
973	
974	  3. **A race-prone scenario in plain English** — without code, describe what would happen WITHOUT the lock and what happens WITH it. This is a teaching dry-run, not a trace; one paragraph max.
975	
976	Format example:
977	> **Scenario 1 — Happy path:**
978	> Initial state: `compartments={A: AVAILABLE, B: AVAILABLE, C: AVAILABLE}`, > `_reservations={}`, `_by_code={}`
979	>
980	> | Step | Call | After |
981	> |---|---|---|
982	> | 1 | `assign_package("LKR1", pkg_med)` | A: AVAILABLE, B: RESERVED, C: AVAILABLE; `_by_code={ "9421": res-1 }` |
983	> | 2 | `deposit_package("res-1")` | B: OCCUPIED; `res-1.status = AWAITING_PICKUP` |
984	> | 3 | `pickup_package("9421")` | B: AVAILABLE; `_by_code={}`; returned package status PICKED_UP |
985	>
986	> Both indexes cleaned up, compartment freed. ✓
987	
988	The verification section bridges §6 (code) and §7 (run) — readers see the design works BEFORE seeing the test output. This is a tutorial-quality step that hellointerview does explicitly and we previously skipped.
989	
990	After the last class, add a **Complexity Summary** table — the interviewer WILL ask this, so have it ready. For every public operation on the orchestrator, list:
991	
992	| Operation | Time Complexity | Space | Key reason |
993	|---|---|---|---|
994	| park(vehicle) | O(log n) | O(1) | heapq push on n available spots |
995	| unpark(ticket_id) | O(log n) | O(1) | dict lookup O(1) + heapq pop O(log n) |
996	| … | … | … | … |
997	
998	Be precise — reference the actual data structure from §5.5-C. This table is what you read out when the interviewer says "what's the time complexity of your design?"
999	
1000	## 7. Putting It Together (verified run + JUnit 5 suite)
1001	TWO deliverables in this section, both run cleanly:
1002	
1003	### 7.1 `main.java` — narrated demo driver
1004	A small `__main__` script that exercises 3-5 happy/tricky cases sequentially with `print()` lines so the candidate (or interviewer) can `mvn compile exec:java -Dexec.mainClass="com.interview.lld.<domain>.Main" -q` and read a human-friendly trace. Each case prints a one-line "Case N <description> -> OK" so a green run is visually obvious. NO assertions hidden in `try/except False` patterns — just `assert X, message` so failures point at the exact line. The driver is for demonstration; rigorous coverage lives in `tests/`.
1005	
1006	### 7.2 `tests/test_<domain>.java` — JUnit 5 suite
1007	Proper JUnit 5 tests under `tests/`. Every public method of the orchestrator gets at least one test; every typed exception path has its own test. Use parametrize for size/state matrices. Use `JUnit 5.raises(<TypedError>)` — never bare `JUnit 5.raises(Exception)`. Inject the clock via a fixture so expiry tests are millisecond-fast. Include:
1008	
1009	  - One test per **happy path** per public operation
1010	  - One test per **typed exception** raised by the orchestrator
1011	  - One **boundary** test (capacity exhausted, empty input, etc.)
1012	  - One **idempotency** test if any method is documented as safe-to-call-twice
1013	  - The **concurrency test** below
1014	
1015	CONCURRENCY TEST — STRICT ASSERTIONS. The race test must verify EXACT post-state, not just count. For an N-thread / K-capacity race:
1016	  - `assert len(wins) == K` — exactly K threads succeeded
1017	  - `assert len(set(wins)) == K` — every winner has a UNIQUE reservation id (no double-booking on the same compartment under a different id)
1018	  - `assert len({w.compartment_id for w in winning_reservations}) == K` — each winning reservation occupies a DIFFERENT compartment (this catches the bug where a race lets two reservations collide on one box even though both "succeeded")
1019	  - `assert len(losses) == N - K` and EVERY loss is the typed `NoCompartmentAvailableError` (no surprise exceptions)
1020	  - `assert <count of in-RESERVED-or-OCCUPIED compartments> == K` (count via the entity's status field, not via a separate set the service maintains)
1021	
1022	The single-count assertion that the previous version had (`len(wins) == 5`) is NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT — it can pass even if all five wins reserved the same box. The unique-compartment assertion is what makes the test trustworthy.
1023	
1024	Then SHOW the actual `JUnit 5 -q` output AND the `mvn compile exec:java -Dexec.mainClass="com.interview.lld.<domain>.Main" -q` output — both must be green. If either errors, fix the code (not the test) and re-run.
1025	
1026	## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)
1027	A `sequenceDiagram` of one important end-to-end flow — choose the most interesting one (the "happy path" through the core operation, e.g. a user booking, a payment going through, a spot being parked). Then provide the following:
1028	
1029	**Which flow and why:** One sentence on why you chose this flow — "I'm showing [operation] because it touches every class in the design and shows how the Strategy pattern actually fires at runtime."
1030	
1031	**🎙️ Script: walk through this diagram arrow by arrow.** The exact words you'd say while pointing at each arrow in the sequence. For each message (arrow), say: (a) which object sends it, (b) which receives it, (c) what happens inside the receiver that matters. Don't describe the diagram — narrate it like you're telling a story: "[Actor] calls `[method]` on [Object]. [Object] doesn't do the work itself — it delegates to [SubObject], which [does X]. The interesting moment is the call to `[specificMethod]` — that's where the Strategy fires. [StrategyImpl] runs its `[method]` and returns [Y]. # Design a Parking Lot (Java 17+, thread-safe, in-memory)

> Mode for this writeup: **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber/Meta style).** I write the code and RUN it. §7 shows real `mvn` output.

## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate "in-memory thread-safe parking lot", propose scope cuts. Don't write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — `park`, `unpark`, plus two read methods. "Here's the contract before I design classes." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — 5 boxes: `ParkingLotService`, `ParkingSpot`, `SpotAllocationStrategy`, `BestFitNearestStrategy`, `ThreadSafeParkingLotService`. Mention `Vehicle`/`Ticket`/`ParkingFloor` verbally. | ✍️ DRAW |
| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — Strategy + Template Method, then: "I see the double-allocation race in `park` — two cars see the same spot free. I'll fix it with per-spot locks at the end." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — key insight: spot is a self-guarding state machine + each spot carries its own lock; selection is a pluggable strategy. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** — enums → `ParkingSpot` state machine + per-spot lock → `SpotAllocationStrategy` + best-fit → `ParkingLotService` skeleton → `park()` → `unpark()`. | ✍️ WRITE |
| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 3** — run `Main`, point at output. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-spot `ReentrantLock`, no-op `withSpotGuard` hook, thread-safe subclass. Write the subclass only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
| 55–60 min | **Follow-ups** — RLock vs Lock, per-spot vs global, distributed CAS. | ❓ IF ASKED |

**Two interview modes:**
- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output, demo verbally.
- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta):** Run `Main` in §7. This writeup is Mode B.

**The one move that flags "strong hire":** right after the diagram, say "I see a double-allocation race in `park`" — before being asked.

---

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)

> 💬 "We're building the brain of a parking garage that lives in memory. Cars, motorcycles, and handicapped vehicles drive up; the system finds them the best free spot across multiple floors, hands back a ticket, and when they leave it frees the spot and charges them for how long they stayed. The two hard parts are picking the *right* spot — smallest one that fits, nearest floor — and making sure two cars arriving at the exact same second never get handed the same spot."

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)

**You:** "For v1 I'll assume a single in-memory lot in one process — no database, no multi-region. The whole thing is one JVM. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep, in-memory is the scope."
*✓ scoped: one process, in-memory state — distributed is a §9 'if we scaled' note.*

**You:** "Spot sizes: I'll model compact, large, and handicapped. A motorcycle fits a compact or larger, a car needs at least a large, and a handicapped vehicle needs a handicapped spot — and a regular car must NOT be allowed to take a handicapped spot. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed — and yes, protect the handicapped spots."
*✓ locked: handicapped is a reserved category matched by exact equality, not by size ranking.*

**You:** "Allocation policy: I'll do best-fit — smallest spot that still fits — breaking ties by nearest floor. I'll make that a pluggable strategy so we can swap to 'cheapest first' or 'random' later without touching core code. Reasonable?"
**Interviewer:** "Love it, make it pluggable."
*✓ Strategy seam is in scope — this is the design's smart move.*

**You:** "Billing: charge by time, per started hour, rounding partial hours up, with a one-hour minimum. I'll keep money in cents to avoid float bugs. Fine?"
**Interviewer:** "Sounds good. Flat hourly rate is fine for now."
*✓ scoped: flat rate, round up, cents. Tiered/peak pricing is a future strategy.*

**You:** "Concurrency is the meat: many cars park and unpark at once. I'll keep the core single-threaded-clean and then show a thread-safe version using a per-spot lock so two cars never double-book one spot. I'll prove it with a 50-thread race test. That's the deliverable you care about?"
**Interviewer:** "Exactly — show me no double-allocation under contention."
*✓ the concurrency angle is per-spot fine-grained locks; the race test is the proof.*

**You:** "I'll treat the physical gate hardware, license-plate OCR, and payment processing as out of scope — we just return a fee amount. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed, out of scope."
*✓ system boundary: we compute the fee, we don't move money or open gates.*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**

1. **A vehicle parks** by driving up with a known type (motorcycle / car / handicapped).
   - System finds the best free *compatible* spot across all floors and marks it occupied
   - Returns a `Ticket` (spot id, vehicle, entry time) on success
   - Throws `NoSpotAvailableException` if no compatible spot is free
2. **A vehicle unparks** by presenting its ticket id.
   - System frees the spot and returns the fee (cents)
   - Throws `TicketNotFoundException` for an unknown or already-used ticket
3. **Allocation is best-fit, nearest-floor** and pluggable.
   - Smallest fitting spot wins; ties broken by lowest floor, then spot id
   - The policy is a Strategy — swappable without core changes
4. **Billing by time** — per started hour, rounded up, one-hour minimum, in cents.
5. **Concurrent park/unpark are safe** — no two vehicles ever get the same spot.
   - N threads parking into K spots → exactly K succeed on K *distinct* spots; the rest get the typed full error
6. **Read queries** — `occupiedCount()`, `availableCountFor(type)`.

**Out of scope**
- Physical gate / barrier hardware (downstream — we just say "occupied")
- Payment processing (we return a fee number; money movement is a separate system)
- License-plate recognition (input is a given `Vehicle`)
- Multi-process / multi-region (in-memory single JVM for v1)

> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK, before I model anything, the one thing I want to lock down is the concurrency contract — N cars racing for K spots must yield exactly K winners on K distinct spots. With that pinned, let me walk through the actors and flows."

---

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**
- **Driver / vehicle** — drives up to park, presents a ticket to leave. Initiates both core flows.
- **Lot operator (config)** — builds the lot (floors and spots) at startup. Not a runtime actor.
- **Billing reader** — whoever reads the fee returned by `unpark` (a kiosk / payment system downstream).

**Core flows**
1. Park: driver arrives → strategy picks best free compatible spot → spot marked occupied → ticket returned
2. Unpark: driver presents ticket → system finds spot → spot freed → fee computed and returned
3. Reject: driver arrives, no compatible spot free → typed `NoSpotAvailableException` (never a silent null)

---

## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough

> **ParkingLot / Lot** — The whole system. It owns the floors, the open-ticket registry, the clock and rate, and coordinates park/unpark. This is the orchestrator. *Accepted — it becomes `ParkingLotService`.*
>
> **ParkingFloor** — A real grouping of spots with a floor number that drives "nearest". It owns its spots (composition). *Accepted.*
>
> **ParkingSpot** — A physical spot with an id, a size, and an occupancy state — AND it carries its own lock. It has data plus behavior (occupy/release as a guarded state machine). This is the heart of the design. *Accepted.*
>
> **Vehicle** — What's parking. We care about its plate (identity on the ticket) and its type (drives compatibility). It never changes after creation. *Accepted — as an immutable record.*
>
> **Ticket** — The entry receipt: spot id, vehicle, entry timestamp. It has its own identity and an immutable lifecycle. Worth its own type so unpark can look it up and billing can read the entry time. *Accepted — immutable record.*
>
> **AllocationStrategy** — "How do we pick a spot?" is a policy that will change. Pulling it behind an interface is the smart move here. *Accepted — Strategy interface.*
>
> **Fee / Price** — A number computed at exit from entry time and rate. It has no lifecycle of its own. *Rejected — it's a `long` (cents) returned by `unpark`, computed by a private helper.*
>
> **Size / VehicleType / Status** — Finite sets of values with a compatibility rule. *Rejected as entities — modeled as enums (`SpotSize`, `VehicleType`, `SpotStatus`). The "fits" rule lives on `VehicleType`.*
>
> **Driver / Customer** — Actors who call the system; we don't store them. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.*
>
> **Gate / Barrier / Payment** — Out of system scope per §1.3. *Rejected — external systems.*

### 3.2 Final entity table

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ParkingLotService` | Coordinate park/unpark; own the truth about who's parked | `floors: List<ParkingFloor>`, `strategy: SpotAllocationStrategy`, `clock: Supplier<Instant>`, `ratePerHourCents: long`, `openTickets: Map<String,Ticket>` (key = spot id), `ticketCounter: AtomicLong` | Every parked car has exactly one open ticket; a freed spot is removed from the registry |
| `ParkingFloor` | Own a labelled bag of spots on one floor | `floorNumber: int`, `spots: List<ParkingSpot>` | Owns its spots; outsiders get an unmodifiable view |
| `ParkingSpot` | Hold one car; guard its own status; carry its own lock | `id: String`, `floorNumber: int`, `size: SpotSize`, `status: SpotStatus`, `vehicle: @Nullable Vehicle`, `lock: ReentrantLock` | A FREE spot can be occupied once; only OCCUPIED→FREE on release; release is a no-op otherwise |
| `Vehicle` | Identify what's parking and its type | `licensePlate: String`, `type: VehicleType` | Non-blank plate, non-null type (validated at construction) |
| `Ticket` | The entry receipt for billing & unpark | `id: String`, `spotId: String`, `vehicle: Vehicle`, `entryTime: Instant` | Immutable; all fields non-null |
| `SpotAllocationStrategy` | Decide which free spot to use (policy) | *(interface)* `findSpot(floors, type): Optional<ParkingSpot>` | Returns a *candidate* only; never mutates state |

**Enums** — `SpotSize {COMPACT, LARGE, HANDICAPPED}` (each carries a `rank`); `VehicleType {MOTORCYCLE, CAR, HANDICAPPED}` (each owns a `fits(spotSize)` rule); `SpotStatus {FREE, OCCUPIED}`.

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro

> 💬 "So the model has six objects. `ParkingLotService` is the brain — it owns the floors and a map of open tickets, keyed by spot id so unpark is O(1) on the spot. `ParkingSpot` is the interesting one: it remembers its own physical state and carries its own lock — it's the only thing allowed to flip itself occupied or free, and to take a spot you lock that one spot. `SpotAllocationStrategy` is the seam — the lot asks it which spot, so the policy is swappable. `Vehicle` and `Ticket` are immutable records — plain data. The pieces I deliberately left OUT are Fee, which is just a number computed at exit, and Driver/Gate/Payment, which live outside our boundary."

---

## 3.5 API / System Interface

| Method | Signature | Returns | Throws | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `park` | `park(Vehicle vehicle)` | `Ticket` — the entry receipt | `ParkingException` (null vehicle), `NoSpotAvailableException` | A driver arrives; system picks the best free compatible spot and issues a ticket |
| `unpark` | `unpark(String ticketId)` | `long` — fee in cents | `TicketNotFoundException` | A driver leaves; system frees the spot and bills by time |
| `occupiedCount` | `occupiedCount()` | `int` — number of parked cars | — | How full is the lot right now (O(1) registry size) |
| `availableCountFor` | `availableCountFor(VehicleType type)` | `long` — free compatible spots | — | How many spots a given vehicle type could still use |

> 🎙️ "The whole system is four public calls — two that change state (`park`, `unpark`) and two read-only queries. Everything else is internal: the spot guards itself, the strategy picks the spot, billing is a private helper. Handed this interface cold, you'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a class."

---

## 4. Class Design

### 4.1 Per-class derivation

#### (1) `ParkingLotService` — the orchestrator

**(a) Role** — The system's public API. External code calls it to park and unpark; it coordinates the strategy and the spots and owns the open-ticket registry.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "find the best free compatible spot across all floors" | The list of floors |
| "allocation is pluggable" | A reference to the allocation strategy |
| "bill by time" | The clock (injected) and the hourly rate |
| "unpark by ticket; never strand a car" | A registry of open tickets, keyed by spot id |
| "readable ticket ids" | A ticket counter |

```
class ParkingLotService:
    - floors: List<ParkingFloor>
    - strategy: SpotAllocationStrategy
    - clock: Supplier<Instant>
    - ratePerHourCents: long
    - openTickets: Map<String, Ticket>   # key = spot id
    - ticketCounter: AtomicLong
```

**State placement trade-off** — Occupancy could live on the orchestrator (a set of taken spot ids) OR on the spot itself. I put the *physical* status (FREE/OCCUPIED + which vehicle) on the `ParkingSpot` because that describes the spot's own condition, and I put the *relational* fact (which ticket maps to which spot) in the orchestrator's registry because that's a system-managed relationship. Physical state on the entity, relational state in the orchestrator.

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "a vehicle parks → ticket or full error" | `park(vehicle) -> Ticket` |
| "a vehicle unparks → fee" | `unpark(ticketId) -> long` |
| "how full is the lot" | `occupiedCount() -> int` |
| "how many spots free for a type" | `availableCountFor(type) -> long` |
| "concurrency seam (no-op in core)" | `withSpotGuard(spot, action) -> T` (protected) |

```
class ParkingLotService:
    - floors: List<ParkingFloor>
    - strategy: SpotAllocationStrategy
    - clock: Supplier<Instant>
    - ratePerHourCents: long
    - openTickets: Map<String, Ticket>
    - ticketCounter: AtomicLong

    + ParkingLotService(floors, strategy, clock, ratePerHourCents)
    + park(Vehicle) -> Ticket | throws NoSpotAvailableException
    + unpark(String ticketId) -> long | throws TicketNotFoundException
    + occupiedCount() -> int
    + availableCountFor(VehicleType) -> long
    # withSpotGuard(spot, action) -> T   (protected hook, no-op here)
    - nextTicketId() -> String
    - findOpenTicketById(String) -> Ticket
    - findSpotById(String) -> ParkingSpot
    - computeFee(Instant, Instant) -> long
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why key `openTickets` by spot id, not ticket id?** Unpark needs to free the *spot*, and a spot can be re-keyed by the next park. Keying by spot id makes "free this spot" O(1) and naturally caps the map at lot capacity.
- **Why inject the clock as a `Supplier<Instant>`?** So tests fast-forward time without sleeping. A 90-minute stay is two array writes, not a 90-minute test.
- **Why is `withSpotGuard` a no-op here?** This file stays 100% lock-free and readable. The thread-safe subclass overrides this one method — the concurrency diff is one method, not a rewrite.

#### (2) `ParkingSpot` — the state machine + lock holder

**(a) Role** — One physical spot. The only class allowed to change its own occupancy, and the carrier of the per-spot lock that prevents double-allocation.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "spots of different sizes" | its `size` |
| "nearest floor" | its `floorNumber` |
| "mark occupied / free" | its `status` and the `vehicle` in it |
| "concurrent park safe per spot" | its own `lock` |

```
class ParkingSpot:
    - id: String
    - floorNumber: int
    - size: SpotSize
    - status: SpotStatus
    - vehicle: @Nullable Vehicle
    - lock: ReentrantLock
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "mark a free spot occupied (guarded)" | `occupy(Vehicle) -> void | throws InvalidSpotStateException` |
| "free a spot (idempotent)" | `release() -> void` |
| "strategy needs to read availability" | `isFree() -> boolean`, `size()`, `floorNumber()`, `id()` |
| "thread-safe subclass needs the lock" | `lock() -> ReentrantLock` |

```
class ParkingSpot:
    - id: String
    - floorNumber: int
    - size: SpotSize
    - status: SpotStatus
    - vehicle: @Nullable Vehicle
    - lock: ReentrantLock

    + ParkingSpot(id, floorNumber, size)
    + occupy(Vehicle) -> void | throws InvalidSpotStateException
    + release() -> void          # idempotent
    + isFree() -> boolean
    + status() -> SpotStatus
    + vehicle() -> Vehicle | null
    + lock() -> ReentrantLock
    + id(), floorNumber(), size() -> getters
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does the spot own its lock instead of the service holding a `Map<spotId, Lock>`?** Co-locating the lock with the thing it guards means there's no separate map to keep in sync and no "lock the map before the lock" dance. The lock's lifetime is exactly the spot's lifetime.
- **Why is `occupy` guarded but `release` idempotent?** Occupying an occupied spot is a real bug (two cars on one spot) — it must throw. Releasing a free spot is a harmless double-free (a real unpark racing a future cleanup sweep) — it must be a silent no-op.

#### (3) `SpotAllocationStrategy` — the seam (interface)

**(a) Role** — The pluggable policy for "which free spot to use". The orchestrator depends on this abstraction, never on a concrete policy.

**(b) State** — none (stateless policy).

**(c) Operations**

| Need | Method |
|---|---|
| "find the best free compatible spot" | `findSpot(floors, type) -> Optional<ParkingSpot>` |

```
interface SpotAllocationStrategy:
    + findSpot(List<ParkingFloor> floors, VehicleType type) -> Optional<ParkingSpot>
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why `Optional`, not null?** "No spot" is a real answer the caller must unwrap; the service turns an empty Optional into a typed `NoSpotAvailableException`. A null could silently slip through.
- **Why return a candidate, not occupy it?** Selection is a pure read; occupying is a guarded write the service does under the lock. Keeping them separate is what lets the *re-check inside the lock* fix the race.

#### (4) `BestFitNearestStrategy` — the default policy

**(a) Role** — The concrete policy: smallest fitting spot, nearest floor, deterministic tie-break.

**(b) State** — one static `Comparator`.

**(c) Operations** — implements `findSpot`.

```
class BestFitNearestStrategy implements SpotAllocationStrategy:
    - BEST_FIRST: Comparator<ParkingSpot>   # size rank, then floor, then id

    + findSpot(List<ParkingFloor> floors, VehicleType type) -> Optional<ParkingSpot>
    - isCompatible(SpotSize, VehicleType) -> boolean
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why sort by `(size, floor, id)` and not just size?** Floor gives "nearest"; id gives a *deterministic* pick so the same lot state always returns the same spot — reproducible tests, no flakiness.

#### (5) `Vehicle` and `Ticket` — immutable records

**(a) Role** — Plain value objects: `Vehicle` is the thing parking; `Ticket` is the entry receipt.

**(b)/(c)** — Records; validated at construction.

```
record Vehicle(String licensePlate, VehicleType type)      # non-blank plate
record Ticket(String id, String spotId, Vehicle vehicle, Instant entryTime)  # all non-null
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why records?** Immutability for free, value equality, no setters — a ticket once issued can never be mutated.
- **Why no `fee` on the ticket?** It's unknown at entry; computed at exit from `entryTime` and the exit time.

#### (6) `ThreadSafeParkingLotService` — the concurrency subclass

**(a) Role** — The thread-safe version. Overrides exactly one method.

**(b) State** — none added.

**(c) Operations** — overrides `withSpotGuard`.

```
class ThreadSafeParkingLotService extends ParkingLotService:
    + ThreadSafeParkingLotService(floors, strategy, clock, ratePerHourCents)
    # withSpotGuard(spot, action) -> T   (override: lock spot, run, unlock)
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why a subclass, not a flag?** The clean core has zero lock code; the diff between clean and safe is one overridden method. The interviewer sees the seam instantly.

- **`BestFitNearestStrategy ..|> SpotAllocationStrategy`** (realization, dashed): the concrete policy *implements* the strategy interface. This is **the extensibility seam** — a new policy is a new class realizing this interface, zero core edits.

- **`ParkingLotService ..> ParkingSpot`** (dependency): the service calls `occupy`/`release` on a spot under the guard, but it does not own spots (floors do).

| `ParkingSpot` (occupy/release) | State machine (Information Expert) | the spot guards its own legal transitions; nobody writes its status directly |

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)

> 💬 "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to the data it has. `ParkingLotService` owns allocation coordination and the open-ticket registry because it's the only thing that sees all floors and all tickets. `ParkingSpot` owns its occupancy and its lock because physical presence — 'is there a car in me right now' — is intrinsic to the spot, not relational to the system. `VehicleType` owns the `fits` rule because compatibility is intrinsic to the vehicle's kind. So nobody reaches across boundaries to mutate state they don't own."

### 5.2 Applied patterns

**Strategy** — `SpotAllocationStrategy` / `BestFitNearestStrategy`
- **Why it fits here:** the rule for picking a spot is *exactly* the thing the business will want to tweak — best-fit today, nearest-door or cheapest-category tomorrow. So I extract the policy and the orchestrator never hardcodes it.
- **Interface + impl:** `SpotAllocationStrategy.findSpot(...) → BestFitNearestStrategy`
- 🎙️ "The lot doesn't decide which spot — it asks a strategy. Swapping the rule is one new class."
- 💬 **Layman:** "It's like the lot doesn't choose the parking space itself — it asks a rule-of-the-day. We can change the rule without rewiring the lot."

**Template Method** — `ParkingLotService.withSpotGuard`
- **Why it fits here:** I want the clean core to have *zero* lock code, and the thread-safe version to differ by as little as possible. A no-op hook in the base, overridden by the subclass, makes the concurrency diff exactly one method.
- **Hook + override:** base `withSpotGuard(spot, action) { return action.get(); }` → subclass wraps it in `spot.lock()`.
- 🎙️ "My core file imports no locks. Thread safety is one overridden method."
- 💬 **Layman:** "I leave a blank slot where a lock would go. The clean version leaves it blank; the safe version fills it in."

### 5.3 SOLID principles

- **Single Responsibility:** `ParkingSpot` owns physical state, `SpotAllocationStrategy` owns selection, `ParkingLotService` owns coordination, `computeFee` owns billing. Four reasons to change, four homes — never bundled.
- **Open/Closed:** a new allocation policy = a new `SpotAllocationStrategy` class, zero edits to the service. A new pricing rule = a new private helper or injected pricer.
- **Dependency Inversion:** `ParkingLotService` depends on the `SpotAllocationStrategy` abstraction and a `Supplier<Instant>` clock abstraction — never on a concrete policy or on `Instant.now()` directly.
- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeParkingLotService` is a drop-in for `ParkingLotService` — it strengthens guarantees (thread safety) without changing the contract.

### 5.4 Earn your patterns

- **Strategy** — real interface (`SpotAllocationStrategy`) with a real impl (`BestFitNearestStrategy`) the service holds via constructor injection. Survives Cmd-F.
- **Template Method** — real `protected withSpotGuard` no-op in §6, real `@Override` in the subclass. Survives Cmd-F.
- I did **NOT** claim Singleton (the lot is just constructed once by `Main`, not enforced as single) or Factory (no enum→object translation worth naming). Calling those out would be name-dropping.

## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach

### A. The Key Insight

> **Insight:** Make each `ParkingSpot` a self-guarding state machine that *carries its own lock*, and make spot selection a swappable Strategy. To park, lock the one spot you're taking, re-check it's free, occupy it.

> **Why it works:** "Which spot is best" is a pure policy with no shared state — it factors cleanly into a Strategy. And "is this spot taken" is intrinsic to the spot, so the spot can guard its own FREE→OCCUPIED transition. Because the lock lives *on the spot*, two cars taking two different spots take two different locks and never block each other. The only contention is two cars fighting for the *same* spot, and the lock plus re-check makes one win cleanly and the other go find another spot.

> **What breaks without it:** The naive version inlines `spot.status = OCCUPIED` in the service after a bare `findSpot`. Under load, two threads both see spot L1-1 free (check-then-act), both write OCCUPIED, and two cars are assigned one spot — one ticket silently overwrites the other in the registry. By making the spot guard its own transition *and* requiring its own lock for the write, that bug is impossible by construction.

### B. Per-Operation Algorithm

#### `park(vehicle)`

**(1) Pseudocode**
```
1. if vehicle is null -> throw ParkingException
2. candidate = strategy.findSpot(floors, vehicle.type)
3. if candidate is empty -> throw NoSpotAvailableException
4. withSpotGuard(candidate):          # no-op in core; per-spot lock in subclass
5.     if candidate is NOT free:       # re-check inside the guard
6.         return park(vehicle)        # spot was taken meanwhile; retry once
7.     candidate.occupy(vehicle)       # FREE -> OCCUPIED, guarded by the spot
8.     ticket = new Ticket(nextId, candidate.id, vehicle, clock.now)
9.     openTickets[candidate.id] = ticket
10.    return ticket
```

> 🎙️ **Walkthrough (race-prone method):** "This method has the check-then-act race — `findSpot` checks, `occupy` acts, and steps 5-9 are the critical section. Step 2 is where the Strategy fires — the service doesn't know it's best-fit. Step 5 is the non-obvious one: I re-check *inside* the guard, because the strategy's answer is a hint that may be stale by the time I hold the lock. If the spot got taken, I retry the whole park, which re-runs the strategy and finds the next-best spot. In §9 the guard becomes a real per-spot lock, and steps 5-9 become atomic."

**(3) Dry run — `park(Vehicle("CAR-1", CAR))`** on a fresh 2-floor lot:

| Step | Action | State change |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | `strategy.findSpot(floors, CAR)` | returns L1-1 (smallest fitting=LARGE, lowest floor, lowest id) |
| 4 | enter guard on L1-1 | (no-op in core / lock held in subclass) |
| 5 | re-check L1-1 free | free ✓ |
| 7 | `L1-1.occupy(CAR-1)` | L1-1.status: FREE → OCCUPIED, vehicle=CAR-1 |
| 8 | build ticket | `tkt-1`, spotId=L1-1, entry=08:00 |
| 9 | index it | `openTickets={ "L1-1": tkt-1 }` |
| 10 | return | caller gets `tkt-1` |

> *Edge probe: what if a second car calls `park` while step 7 is mid-flight?* Without a lock both see L1-1 free at step 5 → both occupy → double-allocation. §9 fixes it: the per-spot lock serializes step 5-9, so the loser's re-check at step 5 sees OCCUPIED and recurses to L1-2.

#### `unpark(ticketId)`

**(1) Pseudocode**
```
1. if ticketId is null -> throw TicketNotFoundException
2. ticket = findOpenTicketById(ticketId)
3. if ticket is null -> throw TicketNotFoundException
4. spot = findSpotById(ticket.spotId)
5. withSpotGuard(spot):
6.     spot.release()                  # OCCUPIED -> FREE (idempotent)
7.     openTickets.remove(spot.id)     # drop registry entry
8. return computeFee(ticket.entryTime, clock.now)
```

> 🎙️ **Walkthrough (completion path):** "Unpark is two guards then a release-and-bill. Step 2-3 reject unknown tickets. Step 6 is idempotent on purpose — if a future cleanup sweep already freed the spot, `release` is a no-op, no spurious error. Step 8 is the only place fee is computed, and it reads the injected clock, so a test can pretend hours passed."

#### `availableCountFor(type)` / `occupiedCount()`

**(1) Pseudocode**
```
occupiedCount:        return openTickets.size                       # O(1)
availableCountFor(t): count spots where free AND t.fits(spot.size) # O(total spots)
```

> 🎙️ **Lookup method:** "`occupiedCount` is an O(1) hit on the registry size — the registry *is* the set of parked cars. `availableCountFor` scans the spots, which is fine at a few hundred spots."

### C. Data Structure Choices

| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative) | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Map<String,Ticket>` keyed by **spot id** | open-ticket registry | unpark must free a *spot*; keying by spot id makes "free this spot + drop it" O(1). Keying by ticket id would force a scan of all spots to find which spot to free | O(1) put/remove; O(open) find-by-ticket-id |
| `ReentrantLock` per `ParkingSpot` | per-spot critical section | a single global lock serializes the whole building; per-spot locks let N cars park into N different spots fully in parallel. Co-located with the spot so there's no separate lock-map to sync | O(1) acquire |
| linear scan + `Comparator.min` in strategy | best-fit selection | n ≤ a few hundred spots per lot; a per-size heap adds sync complexity for zero measurable win at this scale | O(n), n small |
| `AtomicLong` counter | ticket ids | readable ids (`tkt-1`) and safe under many threads; a plain `long++` would race | O(1) |
| `ConcurrentHashMap` | registry backing | lets the thread-safe subclass read/write the registry from many threads without a separate map lock | O(1) |

I chose the **scan** over a per-size heap deliberately. I'd flip to a per-size free-list/heap when a single lot crosses ~a few thousand spots — at that point the scan becomes a measurable hot spot.

### D. Tricky Logic

> **🧠 Claim:** `park` must re-check the spot is free *inside* the guard, not trust the strategy's answer.
> **🔬 Proof:** Thread A's `findSpot` returns L1-1. Before A occupies it, thread B (which also got L1-1) occupies it. If A blindly occupies, two cars share L1-1.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** step 5 in `park` — `if (!candidate.isFree()) return park(vehicle);` — re-checks under the guard and retries, finding the next-best spot.

> **🧠 Claim:** `ParkingSpot.release()` must be idempotent.
> **🔬 Proof:** A real unpark and a future stale-ticket sweep can both decide a spot is done; whoever runs second would hit "illegal FREE→FREE" and wrongly error on the legitimate path.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `release()` only flips when `status == OCCUPIED`; otherwise it returns silently.

> **🧠 Claim:** A regular car must never take a handicapped spot, and vice-versa.
> **🔬 Proof:** Pure rank comparison (`spot.rank >= vehicle.minRank`) would let a CAR grab a HANDICAPPED spot if HANDICAPPED had a high rank, or block handicapped vehicles if it had a low rank.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `VehicleType.fits` special-cases HANDICAPPED with exact two-way equality before falling back to the rank test.

> **🧠 Claim:** Fee must round partial hours up with a one-hour minimum.
> **🔬 Proof:** Integer division `minutes/60` would bill 0 for a 30-minute stay — free parking by accident.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `(minutes + 59) / 60`, then `if (hours == 0) hours = 1`.

> **🧠 Claim:** Billing in `long` cents, never `double`.
> **🔬 Proof:** `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3` in floating point — money drifts.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `ratePerHourCents` is a `long`; the fee is `hours * ratePerHourCents`.

### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example

> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
> ```
> Ticket park(Vehicle v):
>     spot = findAnyFreeSpot(v.type)     # bare lookup
>     spot.status = OCCUPIED             # direct write, no guard, no lock
>     return new Ticket(...)
> ```
> **Why it's wrong:** two failure modes. (1) The direct status write bypasses the spot's state machine — illegal transitions become possible. (2) The find-then-write is check-then-act — under concurrency two callers both see the same spot free and both occupy it. The correct version delegates selection to a Strategy, calls `spot.occupy()` so the entity guards itself, and lifts the check-and-occupy under a per-spot lock with a re-check (§9).

### F. Narration Order for §6

| Order | Class | One-line open |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Enums (`SpotSize`, `VehicleType`, `SpotStatus`) | "Let me start with the vocabulary — sizes, vehicle types, and the fits rule in one place." |
| 2 | `ParkingSpot` (state machine + lock) | "This is the heart — the only class allowed to change a spot's status, and it carries its own lock." |
| 3 | `SpotAllocationStrategy` + `BestFitNearestStrategy` | "The seam — the lot never knows which policy is wired in." |
| 4 | `ParkingLotService.__init__` + skeleton | "Here's the brain. Registry keyed by spot id, clock injected, zero locks." |
| 5 | `ParkingLotService.park` | "Now the interesting part — the pipeline I dry-ran, with the re-check inside the guard." |
| 6 | `ParkingLotService.unpark` | "Unpark is two guards then a release-and-bill." |

#### (b) The 7 golden rules
1. **Roadmap pehle bolo** — three files, enums → spot → service.
2. **Skeleton-then-flesh** — orchestrator gets signatures first, bodies later.
3. **State machine before orchestrator** — `ParkingSpot` is independent; the service imports it.
4. **BOLO while likhing** — "yahan clock inject kar raha hoon", "yeh re-check race ke liye hai".

### `ParkingSpot` — the state machine + lock holder

The only class allowed to change a spot's status, and the carrier of the per-spot lock.

```java
package com.interview.lld.parking.model;

import java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock;
import javax.annotation.Nullable;

public class ParkingSpot {
    private final String id;
    private final int floorNumber;
    private final SpotSize size;
    private SpotStatus status;
    @Nullable private Vehicle vehicle;
    private final ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();   // per-spot lock

    public ParkingSpot(String id, int floorNumber, SpotSize size) {
        this.id = id; this.floorNumber = floorNumber; this.size = size;
        this.status = SpotStatus.FREE; this.vehicle = null;
    }

    public String id() { return id; }
    public int floorNumber() { return floorNumber; }
    public SpotSize size() { return size; }
    public SpotStatus status() { return status; }
    public boolean isFree() { return status == SpotStatus.FREE; }
    @Nullable public Vehicle vehicle() { return vehicle; }
    public ReentrantLock lock() { return lock; }

    public void occupy(Vehicle v) {
        // WHY: guard the transition — a non-free spot cannot be occupied again.
        if (status != SpotStatus.FREE) {
            throw new InvalidSpotStateException("spot " + id + " is not FREE, cannot occupy");
        }
        this.status = SpotStatus.OCCUPIED;
        this.vehicle = v;
    }

    public void release() {
        // WHY: idempotent — releasing a free spot is a safe no-op.
        if (status == SpotStatus.OCCUPIED) {
            this.status = SpotStatus.FREE;
            this.vehicle = null;
        }
    }
}
```

### `SpotAllocationStrategy` + `BestFitNearestStrategy`

The seam, then the default policy.

```java
package com.interview.lld.parking.strategy;

import java.util.List;
import java.util.Optional;
import com.interview.lld.parking.model.ParkingFloor;
import com.interview.lld.parking.model.ParkingSpot;
import com.interview.lld.parking.model.VehicleType;

public interface SpotAllocationStrategy {
    Optional<ParkingSpot> findSpot(List<ParkingFloor> floors, VehicleType vehicleType);
}
```

```java
package com.interview.lld.parking.strategy;

import java.util.Comparator;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.Optional;
import com.interview.lld.parking.model.*;

public class BestFitNearestStrategy implements SpotAllocationStrategy {
    // smallest fitting size, then nearest floor, then id (deterministic)
    private static final Comparator<ParkingSpot> BEST_FIRST =
        Comparator.comparingInt((ParkingSpot s) -> s.size().rank())
            .thenComparingInt(ParkingSpot::floorNumber)
            .thenComparing(ParkingSpot::id);

    @Override
    public Optional<ParkingSpot> findSpot(List<ParkingFloor> floors, VehicleType type) {
        return floors.stream()
            .flatMap(f -> f.spots().stream())
            .filter(ParkingSpot::isFree)
            .filter(s -> type.fits(s.size()))
            .min(BEST_FIRST);
    }
}
```

#### Approach (the one comparison — how `findSpot` locates the right spot)

**Approach 1 — first-free-that-fits (rejected):**
```
for each spot: if free and fits -> return it
```
> **Challenges:** returns *any* fitting spot, so a motorcycle can grab a LARGE spot while a COMPACT sits empty — then a car arrives and there's no LARGE left. It wastes the scarce big spots. No notion of "best".

**Approach 2 — per-size heap of free spots (rejected for this scale):**
```
Map<SpotSize, PriorityQueue<ParkingSpot>> freeBySize
park: pop from the smallest fitting size's heap
unpark: push the freed spot back into its size's heap
```
> **Challenges:** state now lives in two places — the spot's status AND the heaps — and they must stay in sync. Forget to push on unpark and the spot vanishes from availability forever. I'd flip to this only when a lot crosses ~a few thousand spots.

**Chosen approach — scan + min by `(size, floor, id)`:**
```
stream all spots -> filter free & compatible -> min by best-first comparator
```
> **Why it wins:** single source of truth — the spot owns its own status, no heaps to sync. Best-fit falls out of the comparator's first key, nearest-floor from the second, determinism from the third. O(n) but n is a few hundred — microseconds.

#### `park(Vehicle)` — the pipeline

**1. Role:** the core deposit workflow — find the best spot and tie it to a ticket in one guarded step.

**2. Core logic (happy path):**
1. Ask the strategy for the best free compatible spot
2. Enter the per-spot guard
3. Re-check the spot is free (it may have been taken since step 1)
4. Occupy it (the spot guards the transition)
5. Build a ticket, index it by spot id, return it

**3. Edge cases:**
- Null vehicle
- No compatible spot anywhere (lot full)
- Spot taken between selection and occupy (the race)

**4. Code:**
```java
public Ticket park(Vehicle vehicle) {
    if (vehicle == null) {
        throw new ParkingException("vehicle must not be null");
    }
    ParkingSpot candidate = strategy.findSpot(floors, vehicle.type())
        .orElseThrow(() -> new NoSpotAvailableException(
            "no free spot for vehicle type " + vehicle.type()));

    return withSpotGuard(candidate, () -> {
        if (!candidate.isFree()) {        // WHY: re-check inside the guard
            return park(vehicle);          //      spot taken meanwhile -> retry
        }
        candidate.occupy(vehicle);         // FREE -> OCCUPIED, guarded by the spot
        Ticket ticket = new Ticket(nextTicketId(), candidate.id(), vehicle, clock.get());
        openTickets.put(candidate.id(), ticket);
        return ticket;
    });
}
```

**5. Reasoning:** `park` is the pipeline. The interesting line is the re-check `if (!candidate.isFree()) return park(vehicle);` — the strategy's answer is only a *hint* that can go stale between selection and the moment we hold the guard, so I look again under the lock and, if the spot's gone, I re-run the whole thing, which finds the next-best spot. Notice I'm *not* validating the vehicle type inside `park` — that's the strategy's job, which simply returns no spot for an incompatible vehicle. In the clean core `withSpotGuard` is a no-op, so this reads as straight-line code; the only thing that changes for thread safety is that the guard becomes a real per-spot lock. The `occupy` call goes through the spot's own state machine rather than a direct status write, so even if the lock were somehow removed, an illegal double-occupy throws instead of silently overwriting a car.

> 🎙️ Say: "Strategy picks a spot, then inside the guard I re-check it's free and occupy it. The re-check is what beats the race — the spot might've been taken since I looked."
> ⚠️ "What if two cars get the same spot from the strategy?" → "Inside the guard I re-check `isFree`. The loser sees OCCUPIED and recurses to the next-best spot. With the per-spot lock in §9, only one car is ever inside the guard for that spot at a time."

#### `unpark(String)` — the completion path

**1. Role:** free the spot and bill by time.

**2. Core logic:** look up the ticket → find its spot → under the guard, release and drop the ticket → compute the fee.

**3. Edge cases:** null ticket id, unknown/used ticket id, ticket pointing at an unknown spot (corrupt config).

**4. Code:**
```java
public long unpark(String ticketId) {
    if (ticketId == null) {
        throw new TicketNotFoundException("ticketId must not be null");
    }
    Ticket ticket = findOpenTicketById(ticketId);
    if (ticket == null) {
        throw new TicketNotFoundException("no open ticket with id " + ticketId);
    }
    ParkingSpot spot = findSpotById(ticket.spotId());
    withSpotGuard(spot, () -> {
        spot.release();                 // OCCUPIED -> FREE (idempotent)
        openTickets.remove(spot.id());  // so the same ticket can't unpark twice
        return null;
    });
    return computeFee(ticket.entryTime(), clock.get());
}
```

**5. Reasoning:** `unpark` is two guards then a release-and-bill. The `spot.release()` is idempotent, so a real unpark racing a future cleanup sweep is safe — whoever runs first frees the spot, the second call does nothing. I `remove` from the registry inside the same guard so the same ticket id can never be unparked twice. The fee is computed *outside* the guard from the injected clock — releasing the spot has nothing to do with reading the entry time, so I don't hold the lock during billing.

> 🎙️ Say: "Look up the ticket, free the spot, drop it from the registry, then bill. Release is idempotent so a double-free is harmless."
> ⚠️ "What if someone unparks the same ticket twice?" → "The first unpark removes it from the registry, so the second `findOpenTicketById` returns null and we throw `TicketNotFoundException`."

#### Helper methods

```java
private String nextTicketId() {
    return "tkt-" + ticketCounter.incrementAndGet();   // readable, thread-safe ids
}

private Ticket findOpenTicketById(String ticketId) {
    for (Ticket t : openTickets.values()) {            // small set: only parked cars
        if (t.id().equals(ticketId)) return t;
    }
    return null;
}

private ParkingSpot findSpotById(String spotId) {
    for (ParkingFloor floor : floors) {
        for (ParkingSpot spot : floor.spots()) {
            if (spot.id().equals(spotId)) return spot;
        }
    }
    throw new TicketNotFoundException("ticket points at unknown spot " + spotId);
}

private long computeFee(Instant entry, Instant exit) {
    long minutes = Math.max(0, Duration.between(entry, exit).toMinutes());
    long hours = (minutes + 59) / 60;     // round up partial hours
    if (hours == 0) hours = 1;            // one-hour minimum
    return hours * ratePerHourCents;      // cents, no float
}
```

`computeFee` rounds partial hours up with a one-hour minimum, all in `long` cents. The clock is injected so a test fast-forwards time instead of sleeping.

### Verification — full-lifecycle dry runs

**Scenario 1 — Happy path** (`park → unpark`) on a fresh lot, rate 200c/hr:
Initial: all spots FREE, `openTickets={}`

| Step | Call | After |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `park(CAR-1)` | L1-1: FREE→OCCUPIED; `openTickets={ "L1-1": tkt-1 }` |
| 2 | clock += 90 min | (no state change) |
| 3 | `unpark("tkt-1")` | L1-1: OCCUPIED→FREE; `openTickets={}`; returns 400 (2h × 200c) |

Registry cleaned, spot freed, fee correct. ✓

**Scenario 2 — Lot full rejection:** fill all 4 LARGE spots with cars; the 5th car's `strategy.findSpot` returns empty → `park` throws `NoSpotAvailableException`. No spot is touched. ✓

**Scenario 3 — Race (plain English):** Two cars call `park` at once; both get L1-1 from the strategy. *Without* the per-spot lock, both pass the `isFree` re-check and both occupy L1-1 — double-allocation. *With* the lock (§9), the second car blocks until the first releases the lock, then its re-check sees L1-1 OCCUPIED and recurses to L1-2. Two cars, two distinct spots. ✓

| Operation | Time | Space | Key reason |

| `park(vehicle)` | O(n) | O(1) | strategy scans n spots; occupy + index are O(1) |
| `unpark(ticketId)` | O(open + n) | O(1) | scan open tickets (bounded by capacity) + locate spot |

## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

**Which flow and why:** I'm showing `park` because it touches every class — the service, the strategy, and the spot's state machine — and it's where the Strategy fires and the race lives.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor Driver
    participant Svc as ParkingLotService
    participant Strat as BestFitNearestStrategy
    participant Spot as ParkingSpot

    Driver->>Svc: park(vehicle)
    Svc->>Strat: findSpot(floors, type)
    Strat->>Strat: scan free compatible spots then pick best by size floor id
    Strat-->>Svc: Optional of candidate spot
    Svc->>Spot: withSpotGuard then re-check isFree
    Spot-->>Svc: free is true
    Svc->>Spot: occupy(vehicle)
    Spot->>Spot: FREE to OCCUPIED guarded transition
    Svc->>Svc: build ticket then index by spot id
    Svc-->>Driver: Ticket
```

> 🎙️ **Walk the diagram arrow by arrow:** "The driver calls `park` on the service. The service doesn't pick the spot itself — it delegates to `findSpot` on the strategy, which scans free compatible spots and returns the best one by size, floor, then id. That's the Strategy firing. Now the service enters the guard on that spot and re-checks `isFree` — the interesting moment, because the candidate could be stale. With the spot confirmed free, the service calls `occupy`, and the *spot itself* flips FREE→OCCUPIED through its guarded transition — the service never writes the status directly. Finally the service builds the ticket, indexes it by spot id, and hands it back."

> **Why the sequence shows the design is good:** the service coordinates but implements no business logic — selection delegates to the strategy and the status change delegates to the spot's state machine. That's Open/Closed and Information Expert at runtime.
